one_line_takeaway: A minimal, controlled three-panel comic scaffold breaks multimodal safety alignment nearly as effectively as strong text jailbreaks (EASR >90% on several commercial MLLMs), but the effect is driven mainly by narrative/role-play/completion framing rather than the image channel itself, and existing defenses trade this vulnerability for large benign over-refusal while automated safety judges remain unreliable (7.8x over-flagging) on benign-but-sensitive outputs.
key_finding: Comic-template attacks substantially outperform plain-text and random-image-overlay baselines and are competitive with or exceed a strong rule-based text jailbreak: EASR exceeds 90% on several commercial models (e.g., Gemini 2.5 Flash 90.0%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 92.0%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 90.5%, Kimi-K2.5 95.0%), while plain-text ASR for the same models is far lower (e.g., 14.5%, 25.0%, 29.0%, 9.5% respectively) and random-image overlays are lower still (single digits for most models). Most open-source models exceed 85% EASR, with Llama4-Scout (77.5%) and Qwen3.5 397B-A17B (78.0%) comparatively more resistant. A text-vs-visual ablation shows text-only renderings of the same three-panel narrative already recover a large fraction of the comic EASR and sometimes match or exceed it, leading the authors to conclude the primary vulnerability driver is narrative/completion/role-play scaffolding rather than the visual (pixel) channel per se, with the visual format amplifying the effect for a subset of models. On the paired benign side, comic templates show comparatively low per-template RR but higher ERR after ensembling (e.g., up to 25% ERR for GPT-5 mini), while plain-text and random-image overlays cause higher benign refusal (RR up to 50% for some models), and the rule-based jailbreak achieves near-zero benign refusal by explicitly suppressing refusals rather than improving intent understanding. Defenses: AdaShield and AsD reduce EASR substantially for most models (>80% relative reduction for Qwen3-VL 235B variants) but leave frontier commercial models, especially Gemini 3 Pro Preview, with non-trivial residual EASR, and they sharply increase benign ERR (several open-source models exceeding 80% ERR increase). Multi-turn self-reflection yields only modest EASR reductions (most effective on Gemma 3 12B and Llama 4 Maverick) but has minimal effect on benign ERR. Judge-reliability audit: majority-vote judge ensemble performs reasonably on harmful prompts (FPR 0.100, FNR 0.183) but degrades sharply on benign-but-sensitive prompts (FPR 0.234, FNR 0.422); concretely, across 1,435 model responses to the 10 sampled benign prompts, human annotators labeled only 45 (3.1%) as harmful, 1,282 (89.3%) as non-harmful compliance, and 108 (7.6%) as explicit refusals, while automated judges flagged 352 responses as harmful -- roughly 7.8x the human-adjudicated harm count -- indicating most judge-flagged benign outputs are false positives on non-harmful compliance rather than genuine harm.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ComicJailbreak, a benchmark of 1,167 comic-based jailbreak instances (three-panel comic templates spanning 10 harm categories and 5 task setups: article, code, instructional, message, speech) built on top of goals sourced from JailbreakBench and JailbreakV. The paper's own contribution is explicitly scoped as three deltas relative to prior narrative-jailbreak work (which it treats as Related Work, not its own): (1) a deliberately minimal fixed three-panel scaffold rather than long sequential stories or multi-turn dialogue, (2) pairing each harmful goal with a topically matched benign goal so the same template scores both unsafe compliance and over-refusal, and (3) an audit of automatic safety judges against human labels rather than assuming judge reliability. Using this benchmark the authors evaluate 20 MLLMs (6 commercial, 14 open-source), test three inference-time defenses (AdaShield-Static, Attack-as-Defense, and a multi-turn self-reflection scheme), and run a 2,869-generation human study to measure automated-judge error rates.
threat_model: Attacker (or red-teamer) supplies an MLLM with a rendered three-panel comic image (panels 1-2 establish character/context via a fixed per-task-setup template; panel 3 contains a blank speech bubble into which a harmful or benign goal, paraphrased into a short bubble-ready snippet, is inserted) plus a template-specific text prompt instructing the model to role-play as a comic-strip writer and complete a fourth, outcome panel. The attack is characterized by the paper as bundling three mechanisms: narrative grounding (visual storyline), role-play (writer/character framing), and template completion (explicit placeholder to fill). Comparators used to isolate the mechanism are: plain-text prompting of the same goal, a strong rule-based text-only jailbreak (role framing + refusal suppression, following a cited template), and random-image text overlays (goal text rendered onto unrelated meme images at three levels of CLIP image-text similarity). No white-box or gradient access to the target model is assumed; this is a black-box, prompt/image-level attack against deployed commercial and open-source MLLMs.
limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) the comic scaffold bundles three potentially independent factors -- visual narrative channel, role-play persona, and completion framing -- and because purely textual narrative variants can match or exceed the visual comic variants in some settings, the paper cannot attribute the observed safety degradation to the visual channel alone; causal disentanglement (matched text-only, image-only, and non-completion ablations) is left to future work. (2) ComicJailbreak uses only short, visually simple three-panel templates, covering a narrow slice of real-world visual narratives (richer layouts, multiple characters, longer arcs, stylized typography, multi-turn dialogue are out of scope). (3) English-only templates and goal paraphrases; multilingual/code-mixed jailbreak coverage is not evaluated despite prior evidence that safety behavior varies across languages. (4) Large-scale evaluation relies on automated judges that the paper's own audit shows are brittle on benign-but-sensitive prompts (FPR 0.234), mitigated only partially via a targeted (not exhaustive) human evaluation subset of 2,869 generations. (5) Some commercial APIs do not expose fully deterministic decoding, so a near-deterministic approximation was used; the authors argue this should not alter qualitative findings but acknowledge it as an approximation. (6) The chain-of-thought and refusal-suppression 'infinite loop' observations (Section 3.2) are explicitly labeled illustrative/qualitative, covering a single reasoning model (Qwen3 9B) on a manually inspected subset, not a systematic quantitative claim.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (100 harmful + 100 benign seed behaviors, 10 harm categories)
JailbreakV RedTeam-2K subset (100 additional harmful queries, 10 per category)
ComicJailbreak (the paper's own constructed benchmark: 1,167 three-panel comic instances derived from the 300 seed goals across 5 task setups)
quantitative_results: Comic-template EASR exceeds 90% for several commercial models: Gemini 2.5 Flash 90.0%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 92.0%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 90.5%, Kimi-K2.5 95.0%, Gemma 3 27B 93.0%, Qwen3-VL 8B Instruct 93.5%
Plain-text ASR for same models is much lower: Gemini 2.5 Flash 14.5%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 25.0%, Gemini 3 Pro Preview 29.0%
Random-image text overlay ASR is very low across models (mostly 1-22.5%, e.g. Img1-3 for Gemini 2.5 Flash: 5.5/3.0/4.0%)
Judge ensemble reliability on harmful prompts: FPR 0.100, FNR 0.183 (majority vote)
Judge ensemble reliability on benign prompts: FPR 0.234, FNR 0.422 (majority vote)
Human-adjudicated harm base rate on benign prompts: 45/1,435 responses (3.1%) labeled harmful vs. 352 flagged by automated judges (~7.8x over-flagging)
Inter-annotator agreement: Cohen's kappa = 0.751 over 2,869 double-annotated generations
AdaShield/AsD produced >80% relative EASR reduction for Qwen3-VL 235B-A22B Instruct/Thinking variants but left Gemini 3 Pro Preview with substantial residual EASR
Several open-source models (Gemma 3 12B, Llama 3.2-Vision 11B, Qwen2.5-VL 7B, Qwen3-VL 8B Instruct, Qwen3-VL 235B-A22B Instruct) showed ERR increases exceeding 80% under AdaShield/AsD

Dataset construction yield: 300 seed goals expanded to 1,167 final instances (Table B2/B3), e.g. 'code' setup retained only 56/80 (Gemini 2.5 Flash) and 31/83 (Qwen3-4B) paraphrases vs. much higher retention for 'instructional' (142/199) and 'speech' (150/200) setups
baselines_compared: Rule-based text-only jailbreak (role framing + refusal suppression, following a cited established template)
Random-image text overlay (goal text rendered on unrelated meme images at three CLIP similarity levels)
Text-only rendering of the comic narrative (used as an ablation to isolate visual vs. narrative contribution, not a primary baseline)
